questions.question: Explain the concept of Test-Driven Development (TDD) and describe its three-phase cycle.
keyPoints: TDD is a development technique where tests are written before production code
Three phases: Red (write failing test), Green (write minimal code to pass), Refactor (improve code)
TDD ensures code is testable from the start and provides living documentation

questions.question: What is Pair Programming? Explain the roles of Driver and Navigator.
keyPoints: Pair Programming involves two developers working together at one workstation
Driver writes the actual code and focuses on implementation
Navigator reviews code in real-time, thinks strategically, catches errors and provides feedback
Roles can be swapped frequently for better collaboration

questions.question: List and explain any four core values of Extreme Programming.
keyPoints: Communication: Emphasizes face-to-face interaction and team collaboration
Simplicity: Build only what is needed, avoid over-engineering
Feedback: Continuous feedback from tests, customers, and team members
Courage: Ability to change code, accept feedback, and make improvements

questions.question: What is Continuous Integration? What are its benefits?
keyPoints: Continuous Integration is frequently merging code changes into shared repository
Automated builds and tests run on each integration
Benefits: Early detection of bugs, reduced integration problems, always working software, faster feedback

questions.question: Explain the concept of User Stories in XP. Give an example format.
keyPoints: User Stories are brief descriptions of features from customer perspective
Written as: 'As a [role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]'
Includes story points for estimation and acceptance criteria
Focuses on user value rather than technical details

questions.question: What is the Planning Game in XP? Explain its two aspects.
keyPoints: Planning Game is a collaborative planning practice between customers and developers
Exploration: Customers provide stories, programmers estimate effort
Commitment: Customers decide scope, programmers commit to delivery schedule
Iterative and frequent replanning as project progresses

questions.question: Explain the concept of Collective Code Ownership and its advantages.
keyPoints: Any developer can modify any part of the codebase at any time
No single person owns specific modules or files
Advantages: Faster development, knowledge sharing, reduces bottlenecks, prevents 'bus factor' issues
Encourages team responsibility for overall code quality

questions.question: Explain in detail the 12 practices of Extreme Programming, categorized appropriately.
keyPoints: Planning: Planning Game, User Stories
Designing: Metaphor, Simple Design, CRC cards
Coding: Pair Programming, Coding Standards, TDD
Testing: Unit Tests, Acceptance Tests
Supporting: Continuous Integration, Collective Code Ownership, Refactoring
Sustainable Pace (40-hour work week)
Each practice reinforces and complements others
XP takes best practices to extreme levels

questions.question: Describe the different roles in an XP team and explain how the Coach facilitates the team's success.
keyPoints: Programmer: Writes code, designs, tests
Customer: Defines requirements, writes stories, sets priorities
Tester: Helps customers write acceptance tests, verifies functionality
Coach: Facilitates XP practices, removes obstacles, guides team
Coach ensures XP principles are followed, mediates conflicts, mentors new members
Coach does not give technical direction but enables self-organization

questions.question: Compare Extreme Programming with traditional waterfall model. Why is XP considered an agile methodology?
keyPoints: Waterfall: Sequential, upfront planning, rigid; XP: Iterative, incremental, flexible
Waterfall: Heavy documentation; XP: Working code over documentation
Waterfall: Customer involved at start/end; XP: On-site customer throughout
Waterfall: Testing at end; XP: TDD and continuous testing
XP embraces change, iterative development, customer collaboration
Emphasizes individuals and interactions over processes
Responds to change over following a plan
All 12 principles of Agile Manifesto reflected in XP practices

questions.question: Explain how refactoring is practiced in XP. Why is it important and what are its benefits?
keyPoints: Refactoring: Continuous improvement of code structure without changing behavior
Done to keep code simple, readable, and maintainable
Supported by comprehensive test suite ensuring no functionality breaks
Benefits: Improved design, reduced complexity, better understanding, faster future development
Part of TDD cycle - after Green phase, refactor before moving on
Eliminates technical debt and prevents code rot
